chunks.text: By 2050, the globe is predicted to have a population of 9.
7 billion.
How to feed such a rapidly growing population is one of the greatest challenges in human history.
Food production is said to need to increase by 70 percent from today.
But we cannot increase arable land.
Rather, through climate change and desertification, the land suitable for farming is shrinking.
A shift to plant - based foods rich in fiber, cutting extra meat
and eating insects.
These alternatives are being proposed.
Vertical farming is gaining attention.
Inside buildings in cities, vegetables are grown hydroponically.
Without needing extensive farmland, fresh vegetables can be supplied from the urban outskirts.
Gene - editing technology is also hoped for.
Crops that increase yields, resist pests, and endure flood and drought.
It is an issue in which favor and concern involve each other.
The problem of meat is also serious.
The livestock industry emits huge amounts of greenhouse gases.
Cultured meat — meat grown in a factory — may be a convert that removes environmental burden.
The problem of food waste cannot be overlooked.
One - third of food produced in the world is thrown away.
Just reducing this alone could feed many people.
There is something we can do at the individual level too.
Clean up the mess and reduce food loss.
Choose locally produced ingredients.
Gradually reduce meat consumption.
Food is the basis of survival and the core of culture.
The future dining table is built by the sum of choices made by each of us.
Our love for the Earth should be reflected in our food choices too.
